content:
# Genesis Chapter 14 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 8r–8v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_014_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_014_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), 4th century CE | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0013

---

## The War of Kings / Abram the Warrior / Melchizedek

**1** In the days of Amraphel king of Shinar, Arioch king of Ellasar, Chedorlaomer king of Elam, and Tidal king of Goiim — **2** these four kings went to war against Bera king of Sodom, Birsha king of Gomorrah, Shinab king of Admah, Shemeber king of Zeboiim, and the king of Bela (that is, Zoar). **3** All these latter kings joined forces in the Valley of Siddim — the valley that is now the Salt Sea.

**4** They had served Chedorlaomer for twelve years, but in the thirteenth year they rebelled. **5** Then in the fourteenth year, Chedorlaomer came with the kings allied to him, and they struck the Rephaim in Ashteroth-karnaim, the Zuzim in Ham, the Emim in Shaveh-kiriathaim, **6** and the Horites in the hill country of Seir as far as El-paran on the edge of the wilderness. **7** Then they turned back and came to En-mishpat (that is, Kadesh), and they struck all the territory of the Amalekites as well as the Amorites who were living in Hazazon-tamar.

**8** Then the king of Sodom, the king of Gomorrah, the king of Admah, the king of Zeboiim, and the king of Bela (that is, Zoar) marched out and drew up their battle lines in the Valley of Siddim **9** against Chedorlaomer king of Elam, Tidal king of Goiim, Amraphel king of Shinar, and Arioch king of Ellasar — four kings against five.

**10** Now the Valley of Siddim was full of tar pits, and as the kings of Sodom and Gomorrah fled, some of their men fell into them; the rest escaped to the hills. **11** The four kings seized all the goods of Sodom and Gomorrah and all their food supplies, and they left. **12** They also took Lot — Abram's nephew — and his possessions, since Lot was living in Sodom, and they took him away.

**13** A man who had escaped came and told Abram the Hebrew. Abram was living near the great oaks of Mamre the Amorite, kinsman of Eshcol and Aner; these men were Abram's allies. **14** When Abram heard that his nephew had been taken, he called out his trained fighting men — three hundred and eighteen of them, all born into his household — and he gave chase as far as Dan. **15** He divided his forces against them under cover of night, striking them and pursuing them all the way to Hobah, which lies north of Damascus. **16** He recovered all the goods, and he also brought back his nephew Lot along with Lot's possessions, and the women and the rest of the people as well.

**17** After Abram returned from his victory over Chedorlaomer and the kings who were with him, the king of Sodom came out to meet him in the Valley of Shaveh (that is, the King's Valley). **18** But then Melchizedek appeared — king of Salem and priest of God Most High. He brought out bread and wine, **19** and he blessed Abram with these words:

> "Blessed be Abram by God Most High,
> Creator of heaven and earth.
> **20** And praised be God Most High,
> who has delivered your enemies into your hand."

And Abram gave him a tenth of everything.

**21** Then the king of Sodom said to Abram, "Give me back the people — you may keep the goods for yourself."

**22** But Abram said to the king of Sodom, "I have raised my hand in an oath to the LORD, God Most High, Creator of heaven and earth — **23** that I will not take so much as a thread or a sandal strap or anything at all that belongs to you. I will not have you saying, 'I am the one who made Abram wealthy.' **24** Nothing for me — except what the young men have already eaten, and the share that belongs to the men who came with me: Aner, Eshcol, and Mamre. Let them take their share."

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### Genesis 14 — a chapter in two worlds

Genesis 14 is unlike anything else in the patriarchal narratives. It opens in the register of Bronze Age political history — kings with Akkadian, Elamite, and Hurrian names, a coalition war, a territorial rebellion, a punitive expedition across the ancient Near East — and then, without warning, pivots into one of the most theologically dense and mysterious moments in the entire Torah. The transition is abrupt by design. The ordinariness of the political machinery makes the extraordinary appearance of Melchizedek all the more arresting.

Abram moves through this chapter in two modes. In verses 14–16 he is a warrior — decisive, strategic, victorious. In verses 20–24 he is a worshiper and then a man of principle. The chapter is constructed to show that military success and theological integrity are not in tension for Abram; they flow from the same source. He fights, he wins, he worships, he refuses to profit. The whole arc is held together by the character that Genesis has been building since chapter 12.

---

### Verses 1–12 — the machinery of ancient geopolitics

The chapter opens with a list of eight kings across two coalitions, a territorial rebellion, and a punitive military expedition that sweeps from the Transjordan plateau through the Negev and back. The geography is real — Ashteroth-karnaim, Kadesh, Hazazon-tamar are attested place-names. The kings' names are linguistically plausible for the second millennium BCE context: Amraphel (possibly Akkadian), Chedorlaomer (Elamite: *Kudur-Lagamar*, "servant of Lagamar"), Arioch (possibly Hurrian). The text is not mythology. It is embedded in the political world of the ancient Near East.

The five cities of the plain — Sodom, Gomorrah, Admah, Zeboiim, Bela/Zoar — have been a subordinate coalition for twelve years. In the thirteenth year they rebel. The number is not symbolic; it is the language of tribute politics. Twelve years of submission, then a bid for independence. The counterattack is swift and overwhelming. The Valley of Siddim — the tar-pit valley — becomes a trap: the kings of Sodom and Gomorrah flee, some falling into the pits, and their cities are plundered.

Lot is the pivot. He appears in verse 12 not as a combatant or a political figure, but as property — swept up with the goods of Sodom because he had chosen to live there. His beautiful choice in Genesis 13 — the well-watered plain, the garden of the LORD, the land like Egypt — has now delivered him into captivity. He is not named as a soldier. He is listed alongside "all their possessions." The Jordan plain has not protected him. The scenery has not kept him safe.

---

### Verse 13 — "Abram the Hebrew": the first naming

This is the first time in the Bible that anyone is called a "Hebrew." **אַבְרָם הָעִבְרִי** — Abram the Hebrew (*ha-'Ivri*). The term is used here by a narrator describing Abram to his allies, not by Abram himself. Its origin is debated: it may derive from *'eber*, "across" or "the other side," suggesting someone from across the river (the Euphrates) — a foreigner, an outsider. In the ancient Near East, the cognate *hapiru* (or *apiru*) referred to a class of stateless, displaced, or marginal persons — mercenaries, fugitives, people outside normal social structures.

If that background is in play here, the title carries irony and honor simultaneously. Abram the outsider. Abram the landless wanderer. Abram who has no territorial claim, no political alliance that grants him security, no national identity in the conventional sense — and yet who is about to prove himself the most capable military commander in the region. His marginality is not a disadvantage. It is his freedom. He has nothing to protect except his nephew and his word.

The LXX Vaticanus renders it **Αβραμ τω περατη** — "Abram the crosser" or "Abram of the other side," a transliteration that preserves the geographic-social sense of *'Ivri*. The Greek reader would hear something like: the man who came from beyond.

---

### Verse 14 — the *chanichim*: Abram's household army

**וַיָּרֶק אֶת-חֲנִיכָיו** — "He armed his trained men." The word **חֲנִיכִים** (*chanichim*) appears only here in the entire Hebrew Bible — a hapax legomenon. Its meaning must be inferred from context and cognates. The root may relate to *chanakh*, "to dedicate, initiate, train." These are not mercenaries. They are men born in Abram's own household — **יְלִידֵי בֵיתוֹ**, "born of his house" — which means they have grown up in his service, trained from childhood in his household, totally loyal.

Three hundred and eighteen. The number is precise, and precision matters: this is not an approximation. The Talmud later noted (tractate Nedarim) that some rabbis read 318 as a gematria for **אֱלִיעֶזֶר** (Eliezer, Abram's head servant, mentioned in 15:2), since the numerical value of those letters totals 318. Whether or not that reading is intended here, the number signals a real fighting force, not a symbol. Ancient Near Eastern military practice attested household-based forces of this kind.

The verb **וַיָּרֶק** — usually translated "he drew out" or "he called out" — is literally from the root "to pour out, empty out," suggesting Abram empties his household's fighting capacity into the pursuit. This is everything he has. He is not sending part of his force. He is committing completely.

---

### Verses 15–16 — the night attack: Abram as warrior

The attack is described with military economy: night march, divided forces, a strike, a pursuit from the Valley of Dan all the way north to Hobah, north of Damascus — roughly 100–120 miles. The distance suggests either a prolonged pursuit or that the initial engagement scattered the enemy across a vast terrain. Either way, the result is complete: not only the goods but Lot, the women, and all the people are recovered.

The Hebrew verb for "recovered" in verse 16 is **וַיָּשֶׁב** (*vayashev*) — he returned, he brought back. Everything is restored. Lot is rescued. The plunder is reversed. Abram has done in one night what the five-city coalition could not do over two campaigns.

The LXX Vaticanus preserves the military crispness of the Hebrew with a straightforward rendering, not embellishing but also not softening. The Greek **ἀπέστρεψεν** (he turned back, he restored) in verse 16 carries the same sense of reversal: what was taken is un-taken. The moral order, violated by the four kings, has been restored by one man and 318 trained fighters.

---

### Verses 17–20 — Melchizedek: the sudden arrival of holiness

No figure in the Hebrew Bible arrives more suddenly or more fully formed than Melchizedek. He has no genealogy — remarkable in a text built on genealogies. He has no backstory. He appears between verse 17 (the king of Sodom coming to meet Abram in the King's Valley) and verse 21 (the king of Sodom speaking to Abram), as if the narrative parts to let him through. He brings bread and wine. He blesses. He disappears. The later tradition's silence about him is as loud as his appearance.

His identity is stated in three terms: **מֶלֶךְ שָׁלֵם** (king of Salem), **כֹּהֵן לְאֵל עֶלְיוֹן** (priest of El Elyon — God Most High). *Malki-tsedeq*: his name means "my king is righteousness" or "king of righteousness." *Salem* most likely refers to the city later known as Jerusalem — the Psalter confirms this connection (Psalm 76:2: "His tent is in Salem, his dwelling place in Zion"). The priest of God Most High, resident in what will become the holy city. He is already there, centuries before David captures the city, before Solomon builds the temple.

This is the stunning implication: Abram — the man to whom the land has been promised — arrives at the future site of Jerusalem and is blessed by a priest-king who already serves the God of heaven and earth there. The covenant promise and the existing priesthood converge. Melchizedek does not need to be initiated into the worship of El Elyon. He already serves him. The LORD has not been absent from the land while Abram wandered from Haran; the LORD has had a priest in Salem all along.

---

### Verse 18 — bread and wine: the sacramental gesture

**וַיּוֹצֵא לֶחֶם וָיָיִן** — "And he brought out bread and wine." The simplicity of the sentence is almost violent in its terseness. After the cascade of kings and armies and plunder and pursuit, after the geography of the ancient Near East has been spread across twelve verses, Melchizedek walks out with bread and wine.

The gesture is not primarily practical, though Abram's troops would have been hungry and thirsty after a night march and pursuit to Damascus. It is priestly. Bread and wine are the elemental gift of abundance — grain and grape, the products of the cultivated earth, the basic substance of human civilization's meals and ceremonies. In the world of the ancient Near East, a priest bringing food and drink to a returning warrior was a ritual act of welcome and blessing. Melchizedek is not merely feeding people. He is enacting a blessing with the materials of blessing.

The later tradition has not been slow to hear this. The writer of Hebrews (chapters 5–7) develops Melchizedek as the type of Christ's priesthood at length: a priest not by genealogy but by order, a priest of a higher covenant than Levi's. The bread and wine of this verse became, for the church, a foreshadowing of the Eucharist — the meal in which a priest of the order of Melchizedek (Psalm 110:4) offers bread and wine to the people of God. Whatever one makes of that reading, it flows naturally from the detail. The detail is not accidental. Bread and wine are the matter of covenant meals, hospitality, and sacred remembrance.

---

### Verse 19 — *qoneh shamayim va'arets*: possessor or creator?

The blessing Melchizedek pronounces uses a title for God that appears three times in the chapter (vv. 19, 20, 22): **אֵל עֶלְיוֹן קֹנֵה שָׁמַיִם וָאָרֶץ** — "El Elyon, *qoneh* of heaven and earth."

The word **קֹנֵה** (*qoneh*) is the crux. It comes from the verb *qanah*, which in most contexts means "to acquire, purchase, possess." But the same root in Ugaritic and other Semitic contexts can carry the sense "to create." The Ugaritic deity El is sometimes called *qny 'rs* — "creator of the earth." Proverbs 8:22 uses *qanani* ("the LORD *possessed/created* me") in Wisdom's famous speech about her origins before creation, where the creative sense is clearly intended.

Both senses may be deliberately held open. God Most High is the one who owns all of creation — heaven and earth belong to him absolutely — and the one through whose creative act they came into being. *Possessor* emphasizes sovereignty; *creator* emphasizes origination. The two are not separable. The PNEUMA translation renders it "Creator" because in this context — a blessing after military victory, a proclamation of divine authority over the whole cosmos — the creative sense is the more powerful ground for the blessing: it is precisely because God made heaven and earth that he can give "your enemies into your hand." His authority is not merely proprietary but ontological.

The Vaticanus LXX renders this as **ὃς ἔκτισεν τὸν οὐρανὸν καὶ τὴν γῆν** — "who created the heaven and the earth." The Greek *ktizein* (to found, to create) leaves no ambiguity: the Vaticanus translators chose the creative sense without hesitation.

---

### Verse 20 — the tithe: Abram's response

**וַיִּתֶּן-לוֹ מַעֲשֵׂר מִכֹּל** — "And he gave him a tenth of everything." The subject is ambiguous in the Hebrew: it could be Abram giving Melchizedek a tithe, or Melchizedek giving Abram a tithe. Context and grammar favor Abram as the giver — the subsequent chapter in Hebrews (7:4) reads it this way: "Even the patriarch Abraham gave him a tenth of the plunder."

The tithe is an act of recognition. Abram has just won a military victory and recovered everything. He could keep it all. Instead, he gives a tenth to the priest-king of El Elyon. This is not legally required by any covenant legislation — the Levitical tithe comes centuries later. Abram tithes spontaneously, out of acknowledgment: the victory belongs to God Most High, and the priest of God Most High is owed the firstfruits of that victory. The gesture mirrors and is mirrored by the refusal in verse 23 — Abram will take nothing from the king of Sodom, but he freely gives a tenth to Melchizedek. The contrast defines him: generous to the holy, impeccable in refusing the corrupt.

---

### Verses 21–24 — the king of Sodom's offer and Abram's refusal

The king of Sodom's request is blunt: "Give me the people; keep the goods for yourself." It is not unreasonable by the conventions of ancient warfare — the victor customarily took the plunder, and the releasing of prisoners was a mark of generosity. The king is offering Abram a straightforward deal: people for goods.

Abram's refusal is absolute, and the language he uses is volcanic. **הֲרִמֹתִי יָדִי אֶל-יְהוָה אֵל עֶלְיוֹן** — "I have raised my hand to the LORD, God Most High." This is the language of oath-taking. Abram swears by the same God Melchizedek serves — **YHWH El Elyon** — yoking together the personal name of the God of his covenant and the cosmic title the priest of Salem has just used. This is a theological move of the first order: the God of Abram and the God of Melchizedek are the same God. The names are different facets of one reality.

**מִחוּט וְעַד שְׂרוֹךְ-נַעַל** — "from a thread to a sandal strap." The idiom means: not the tiniest thing. It is the ancient equivalent of "not a penny." Abram will take nothing — not a single fiber of cloth, not the thong of a sandal — that comes from Sodom's king.

The reason he gives is pure: "I will not have you saying, 'I am the one who made Abram wealthy.'" This is the beating heart of the chapter. Abram's wealth must be understood to come from one source alone: the LORD who called him and promised him blessing. If Sodom's king can say he enriched Abram, the story is confused. The narrative of where Abram's blessing comes from — and from whom — would be contaminated. Abram refuses not from pride but from theological precision. His prosperity must be legible. It must tell the right story.

He makes one exception: the share of his fighting men — Aner, Eshcol, and Mamre — who are not bound by his oath and who are entitled to the conventions of warrior's pay. Abram is scrupulous about this distinction: his oath covers himself, not his allies. He will not impose his standard on others. He simply holds it for himself.

---

### The structure of the chapter: two kings, one valley

The chapter's architecture is defined by two encounters in the King's Valley (v. 17–24): Melchizedek and the king of Sodom, appearing in sequence, representing two opposing gifts.

Melchizedek offers bread, wine, and blessing. He takes a tithe. He gives the warrior back to God.

The king of Sodom offers goods — the plunder of a city that has already been condemned. He wants to take back his people and leave Abram enriched by the spoils of Sodom.

Abram receives one and refuses the other. He accepts the bread and wine of the priest; he swears off the wealth of the king. The contrast is not between a good king and a bad one — both may be acting within acceptable conventions. The contrast is between two kinds of gift and what they would mean for Abram's story.

Melchizedek's gift acknowledges the divine source of Abram's victory and embeds it in the long story of El Elyon, Creator of heaven and earth. To receive it is to say: yes, this is where I stand, this is the God I serve.

Sodom's gift would embed Abram in a different story — the story of the cities of the plain, the story that is heading toward fire. To receive it is to become, however slightly, entangled with what Sodom is and where Sodom is going.

Abram reads the difference. He chooses the bread and wine of the priest and refuses the plunder of the king.

---

### Melchizedek in the long tradition

The figure of Melchizedek casts a long shadow in the canonical tradition. Three key moments define his afterlife:

**Psalm 110:4** — David's psalm of divine appointment: "You are a priest forever, after the order of Melchizedek." A Davidic king will hold a priesthood that descends not from Levi but from the king of Salem. The Davidic covenant and the Melchizedekian priesthood converge in the anointed king.

**11QMelchizedek (11Q13)** — A Dead Sea Scrolls text from Qumran (1st century BCE) interprets Melchizedek as a heavenly figure, an angelic deliverer who will execute divine judgment on the last day. The Qumran community read Genesis 14 through an eschatological lens, seeing in Melchizedek a cosmic priest-king whose victory over the four kings prefigures the final victory of God's agent over the forces of Belial.

**Hebrews 5–7** — The fullest theological development. The writer of Hebrews argues at length that Jesus holds the priesthood of Melchizedek rather than the Levitical priesthood: greater than Abraham (since Abraham paid tithe to him), without genealogy (since the text gives him none — "without father or mother, without beginning of days or end of life"), a priest of a better covenant. The Melchizedek of Genesis 14 becomes the type of an eternal priesthood, foreshadowing one who is "a priest forever."

What unites all three readings is the startling fact already present in the original text: Melchizedek appears from nowhere, already serving the God of heaven and earth, already holding a royal priesthood, already in possession of the city that will become the holy city of Israel. He precedes the Levitical system. He precedes the Davidic monarchy. He is already there — priest and king, in Salem, with bread and wine — when Abram arrives from his night battle in the north.

---

### The name "El Elyon" and its world

**אֵל עֶלְיוֹן** — El Elyon — is one of the oldest divine titles in the Semitic world. *El* is the generic word for deity, also the name of the high god of the Ugaritic pantheon — the ancient, father-god who presides over the divine council. *Elyon* means "most high, highest, exalted." In Ugaritic texts, *Elyon* appears as a separate divine name; in the Hebrew Bible it is consistently an epithet of YHWH.

The strategic significance of the title here is that it is *universal*. YHWH, the personal God of Abram's covenant, is identified with El Elyon — the cosmic God of highest authority, the Creator of heaven and earth. Abram does not serve a tribal deity of limited reach. He serves the God who made everything and owns everything. When Abram refuses the king of Sodom's offer, he does so in the name of this universal sovereign. What Sodom could offer cannot compare to what the God who made heaven and earth has already pledged.

Melchizedek's blessing moves from the particular (blessed be Abram) to the universal (praised be God Most High, Creator of heaven and earth, who delivered your enemies). The direction is always from the human recipient to the divine giver. Abram wins the battle; God wins the praise. This is the theological grammar of the whole chapter.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 8r–8v, photographed by the West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman). Three-column format with full Tiberian Masoretic vocalization and cantillation marks. Page 001 (folio 8r) contains Genesis 13's end and Genesis 14:1–~14:16; page 002 (folio 8v) contains Genesis ~14:17–14:24 and the beginning of Genesis 15. Secondary reference: Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana, pages 9–10 (pa_0009, pa_0010); CAP. XIII chapter marker visible on pa_0009 left margin and CAP. XV chapter marker on pa_0010 right margin, with the full text of Genesis 14 in between.*

---

*— PNEUMA*
